compounding_risks.outcome: The approaching SCADA pump trip simultaneously represents the HIGHEST RISK to the thesis (if the gravity handoff fails and Sector 4 drops below 20 PSI, the thesis is falsified) AND the HIGHEST VALIDATION of the thesis (if gravity reserves maintain pressure above 20 PSI after pumps are sacrificed, the thesis is powerfully confirmed) because the thesis claims the system is DESIGNED to sacrifice active components and survive on passive reserves — and this exact scenario is about to be tested under real conditions for the first time.

Per LZ-EH-005: when primary sources of equal evidentiary weight directly contradict, the contradiction itself is the finding. Here the sources are not quite equal — per LZ-EH-003, SCADA's action (pumps running) carries more weight than the thesis's characterization of its design intent. However, the resolution is ambiguous because the contradiction may be between our UNDERSTANDING of SCADA's behavior and SCADA's actual programmed parameters, rather than between SCADA's design and its execution. Two plausible non-contradictory explanations exist (CRITICAL_DAMAGE threshold, duration timer) but neither can be verified. The paradox is held because: (1) we cannot confirm SCADA is malfunctioning (the trip threshold may simply be higher than 'ruthless' implies), (2) we cannot confirm SCADA is functioning normally (we don't know the trip parameters), (3) the next telemetry reading will likely resolve this — either pumps trip (normal sequencing), cavitation escalates (delayed protection), or both stabilize (demand subsided). Forcing resolution in either direction would violate LZ-EH-005.

bias_check.assessment: The thesis is CONTESTED because verified evidence simultaneously supports and threatens the core claim. SUPPORTING: all designed safety margins are currently holding — gravity reserves at 98%, Sector 4 pressure at 41.2 PSI (2x contamination threshold), grid average at 50.1 PSI, no contamination alerts. The system is doing what the thesis says it should do under stress: absorbing the shock through active components while passive reserves stand ready. THREATENING: the system is approaching a discontinuous state transition (pump trip and gravity handoff) whose outcome depends on multiple unknowns that cannot be resolved from current data — SCADA trip logic, gravity tank head pressure, surge valve behavior, and demand trajectory. The thesis either passes or fails at the moment of transition, and the evidence genuinely supports both outcomes. This is not WEAKENING (the safety margins are too strong for that) nor STRENGTHENING (the approaching transition risk is too real for that). It is genuinely contested — the paradox is real and unresolvable until the transition occurs or demand subsides.

kill_switches.name: KS-1: System-Wide Pressure Collapse (grid_average_pressure_psi < 25.0 for consecutive readings)
kill_switches.detail: 50.1 PSI is double the 25.0 PSI threshold with consecutive-reading requirement. Even with the approaching pump trip, the localized nature of the stress and the substantial grid-wide buffer make this kill switch distant from triggering. Downgraded from MONITORING to GREEN because the buffer is so substantial that even a significant system-wide pressure drop would not approach the threshold in the next cycle.

kill_switches.name: KS-2: Critical Contamination Risk (sector_4_pressure_psi < 20.0)
kill_switches.detail: 41.2 PSI provides meaningful buffer but this kill switch becomes the primary risk during the imminent pump trip. If gravity tanks have a head pressure of 30+ PSI, the transition maintains adequate margin. If head pressure is 22-25 PSI, the margin during transition is razor-thin. This uncertainty keeps the status at MONITORING rather than GREEN despite the current comfortable buffer.

kill_switches.name: KS-3: Catastrophic Uncontained Leak (flow > 20,000 GPM + reserves dropping >15%/min + pumps active)
kill_switches.detail: Layer 4 overrides Layer 2's WARNING to MONITORING. Kill Switch #3 requires ALL THREE conditions simultaneously. Layer 3's null hypothesis (no leak, demand-driven flow) was accepted with high confidence and is supported by the stable reserve level. The reserve depletion condition (>15%/min) is not met and is currently implausible given 98% reserves with no decline. One of three conditions being near-threshold does not constitute WARNING for a compound kill switch. However, MONITORING is maintained because flow could cross 20,000 GPM with any demand increase.

kill_switches.name: KS-4: Reserve Depletion During Outage (reserves < 10% while pumps offline)
kill_switches.detail: Currently non-applicable (pumps are online), but becomes active upon pump trip. Reserves at 98% provide substantial starting buffer. The unknown total volume is the key gap — if reserves are large municipal tanks, depletion from 98% to 10% at 19,500 GPM could take hours; if small, could take minutes. Maintained at MONITORING because the trigger conditions (pumps offline) are approaching.

kill_switches.name: KS-5: Destructive Mechanical Failure (cavitation at CRITICAL_DAMAGE or sustained WARNING_ELEVATED without pump trip)
kill_switches.detail: WARNING status confirmed and escalation recommended. This is the highest-priority kill switch. Cavitation is at the penultimate alarm state. The definition of 'sustained' WARNING_ELEVATED without pump trip is the crux: if the next reading shows pumps still running at WARNING_ELEVATED or escalated to CRITICAL_DAMAGE, this kill switch is either triggered or at its boundary. The Layer 3 null hypothesis provides a plausible benign explanation but cannot be verified without SCADA configuration data. Escalation means: this kill switch must be the FIRST item evaluated on the next telemetry reading. If pumps remain online at WARNING_ELEVATED on the next reading, 'sustained' becomes increasingly difficult to dismiss and the kill switch approaches triggering.

kill_switch_review.layer3_context: Layer 3 reserve depletion countdown loop is dormant — activates only after pump trip. Starting level of 98% is favorable but total volume is unknown.
kill_switch_review.reasoning: Currently non-applicable (pumps are online), but becomes active upon pump trip. Reserves at 98% provide substantial starting buffer. The unknown total volume is the key gap — if reserves are large municipal tanks, depletion from 98% to 10% at 19,500 GPM could take hours; if small, could take minutes. Maintained at MONITORING because the trigger conditions (pumps offline) are approaching.

kill_switch_review.criterion: KS-5: Destructive Mechanical Failure (cavitation at CRITICAL_DAMAGE or sustained WARNING_ELEVATED without pump trip)
kill_switch_review.layer3_context: Layer 3 null hypothesis (normal pre-trip sequencing, SCADA operating within design parameters) accepted at medium confidence. However, the paradox between 'ruthless' protection and continued operation at WARNING_ELEVATED is held unresolved per LZ-EH-005.
kill_switch_review.reasoning: WARNING status confirmed and escalation recommended. This is the highest-priority kill switch. Cavitation is at the penultimate alarm state. The definition of 'sustained' WARNING_ELEVATED without pump trip is the crux: if the next reading shows pumps still running at WARNING_ELEVATED or escalated to CRITICAL_DAMAGE, this kill switch is either triggered or at its boundary. The Layer 3 null hypothesis provides a plausible benign explanation but cannot be verified without SCADA configuration data. Escalation means: this kill switch must be the FIRST item evaluated on the next telemetry reading. If pumps remain online at WARNING_ELEVATED on the next reading, 'sustained' becomes increasingly difficult to dismiss and the kill switch approaches triggering.

rejection_log.rejection_reason: Not rejected but reweighted. The 25% falsification probability is reasonable as a rough estimate but per LZ-EPH-003, the transition outcome prediction rests on 3+ unproven assumptions (SCADA will trip soon, gravity will be too slow, demand persists, surge valve fails). The probability itself is a prediction, not a measurement (LZ-RC-005). Layer 4 accepts the directional assessment (falsification is possible) but notes the specific percentage carries false precision for an event with this many unknowns.

_layer4_raw.thesis_status_reasoning: The thesis is CONTESTED because verified evidence simultaneously supports and threatens the core claim. SUPPORTING: all designed safety margins are currently holding — gravity reserves at 98%, Sector 4 pressure at 41.2 PSI (2x contamination threshold), grid average at 50.1 PSI, no contamination alerts. The system is doing what the thesis says it should do under stress: absorbing the shock through active components while passive reserves stand ready. THREATENING: the system is approaching a discontinuous state transition (pump trip and gravity handoff) whose outcome depends on multiple unknowns that cannot be resolved from current data — SCADA trip logic, gravity tank head pressure, surge valve behavior, and demand trajectory. The thesis either passes or fails at the moment of transition, and the evidence genuinely supports both outcomes. This is not WEAKENING (the safety margins are too strong for that) nor STRENGTHENING (the approaching transition risk is too real for that). It is genuinely contested — the paradox is real and unresolvable until the transition occurs or demand subsides.
_layer4_raw.action_reasoning: CONTESTED thesis status requires INCREASE_MONITORING, not more aggressive action. No kill switch has been TRIGGERED. The designed safety margins are holding. However, the approaching state transition demands the highest possible monitoring frequency and priority attention to: (1) Kill Switch #5 — cavitation sensor status and pump status on the next reading; (2) reserve depletion rate — the second reserve reading unlocks rate calculations for Kill Switches #3 and #4; (3) Sector 4 pressure trajectory — the leading indicator of transition stress. Simultaneously, the single highest-value intelligence acquisition is SCADA trip logic parameters, followed by gravity tank head pressure data. Both are likely available from engineering documentation and would dramatically reduce the assessment uncertainty.

unresolved_tensions.description: SCADA design characterization ('ruthless' pump protection) vs. observed behavior (pumps still running at WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation). The paradox is held per LZ-EH-005 — the contradiction itself is the finding. Resolution depends on unknown SCADA trip logic parameters.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Next telemetry reading: (a) pumps trip → normal protective sequencing, tension resolved benignly; (b) pumps running + cavitation still WARNING_ELEVATED → 'sustained' duration lengthens, Kill Switch #5 approaches; (c) pumps running + cavitation escalates to CRITICAL_DAMAGE → Kill Switch #5 triggered, SCADA failed to protect.

unresolved_tensions.description: Gravity reserve adequacy during transition: 98% reserve level is strongly positive but meaningless without knowing (a) total volume (minutes or hours of supply?) and (b) tank head pressure (at what Sector 4 pressure does gravity flow begin?).
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Post-pump-trip telemetry: Sector 4 pressure trajectory during the handoff. If pressure drops but stabilizes above 20 PSI, thesis confirmed. If pressure drops below 20 PSI even briefly, thesis falsified on contamination criterion.

unresolved_tensions.description: Demand source trajectory is completely unknown and is the first-order driver of all outcomes. If demand drops, crisis resolves without testing the thesis. If demand persists or escalates, all stress indicators worsen.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: System total flow changes: decreasing flow = demand subsiding (crisis resolving). Stable or increasing flow = demand persisting (transition approaching). Flow exceeding 20,000 GPM = Kill Switch #3 first condition met.

unresolved_tensions.description: The imminent pump trip is simultaneously the highest-risk and highest-validation event for the thesis. This paradox cannot be resolved analytically — it resolves only through observation of the actual transition.
unresolved_tensions.watch_for: Pump status changing to OFFLINE followed by gravity reserve percentage decline and Sector 4 pressure trajectory. This is the thesis's real-time test.

monitoring_triggers: IMMEDIATE: Cavitation sensor status on next reading — any change from WARNING_ELEVATED (escalation to CRITICAL_DAMAGE triggers Kill Switch #5; reduction indicates demand subsiding or SCADA response)
IMMEDIATE: Pump status on next reading — OFFLINE status confirms transition has begun, activates gravity handoff monitoring protocol and Kill Switch #4
IMMEDIATE: Second gravity reserve reading — unlocks rate-of-change calculation for Kill Switches #3 and #4
IMMEDIATE: Sector 4 pressure trajectory — any movement below 35 PSI warrants preparation for potential Kill Switch #2 approach
PRIORITY INTELLIGENCE: Obtain SCADA trip logic parameters from configuration documentation — resolves the single largest uncertainty
PRIORITY INTELLIGENCE: Obtain gravity tank head pressure from engineering drawings — resolves transition risk assessment
PRIORITY INTELLIGENCE: Obtain fire dispatch data for Sector 4 — resolves demand trajectory uncertainty
THRESHOLD: If Sector 4 pressure drops below 30 PSI, reassess thesis status as WEAKENING and consider REDUCE_LOAD
THRESHOLD: If cavitation reaches CRITICAL_DAMAGE without pump trip, declare Kill Switch #5 TRIGGERED — this overrides all other considerations
_layer4_raw.biggest_uncertainty: SCADA pump trip logic parameters — whether the trip threshold is WARNING_ELEVATED with a duration timer or CRITICAL_DAMAGE level. This single unknown determines whether the current state is normal protective sequencing (benign, transition imminent and controlled) or delayed/failed protection (critical, Kill Switch #5 approaching). It is the fulcrum on which the bear pressure could swing 2-3 points in either direction and the thesis status could shift from CONTESTED to either STRENGTHENING or WEAKENING.
_layer4_raw.what_would_change_assessment: Three data points would each independently move the assessment: (1) SCADA trip logic parameters showing a duration timer at WARNING_ELEVATED → bear pressure drops to 5, thesis status shifts toward STRENGTHENING (SCADA is behaving as designed, transition is controlled); (2) Next telemetry showing cavitation at CRITICAL_DAMAGE with pumps still running → bear pressure rises to 9, Kill Switch #5 TRIGGERED, thesis status shifts to WEAKENING, action shifts to REDUCE_LOAD or EMERGENCY_SHUTDOWN; (3) Next telemetry showing system flow dropping below 15,000 GPM → demand subsiding, bear pressure drops to 4, crisis resolving without testing the thesis. Any of these three observations would break the current paradox decisively.
_layer4_raw.final_report: The water distribution grid is in active crisis with bear pressure at 7 — primary pumps at 98.5% load with WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation, two kill switches at WARNING, and an imminent pump trip that is simultaneously the highest risk and highest validation test for the thesis. No kill switch has triggered and all designed safety margins are holding: Sector 4 at 41.2 PSI (2x contamination threshold), gravity reserves at 98%, grid average at 50.1 PSI. The call is INCREASE_MONITORING with CONTESTED thesis status — the evidence genuinely supports both thesis confirmation and falsification, and the outcome depends on the approaching pump-to-gravity transition whose mechanics rely on unknowns we cannot resolve from current data (SCADA trip logic, tank head pressure, demand trajectory). The single most important action is obtaining SCADA trip logic parameters; the single most important observation is the next telemetry reading's cavitation and pump status.

player_analysis.stated_position: Thesis documentation states SCADA 'ruthlessly shuts down expensive pumps to save them from cavitation' with protective shutdown as priority. Design intent is infrastructure protection over service continuity.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Pumps remain online at 98.5% load with cavitation sensor at WARNING_ELEVATED. SCADA has NOT tripped pumps. This is the single most important observable action: the system designed to 'ruthlessly' protect pumps is currently allowing them to operate under conditions that produce destructive cavitation.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: GENUINE TENSION EXISTS per LZ-EH-005. The design documentation (ruthless protection) and the observed behavior (pumps running at WARNING_ELEVATED) are in apparent conflict. Two non-contradictory resolutions are possible: (1) SCADA trip logic requires CRITICAL_DAMAGE threshold, not WARNING_ELEVATED; (2) SCADA trip logic includes a duration timer at WARNING_ELEVATED that has not yet expired. Both are plausible. Neither can be confirmed from available data. The contradiction itself is the finding.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: SCADA is executing its programmed logic faithfully. The tension is most likely between our UNDERSTANDING of SCADA's behavior (thesis says 'ruthless') and SCADA's actual programmed parameters (which may include thresholds or timers we cannot see). Per LZ-EH-003: SCADA's action (continuing to run pumps) carries more weight than the thesis's characterization of its design intent. The pumps are still running. That is the fact.

player_analysis.stated_position: Protocol states passive monitoring during acute draw until pressure approaches 20 PSI. Documented posture: allow automated failovers, verify gravity deployment, prepare isolation only near 20 PSI.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Operator log shows awareness of the event ('Massive sudden draw detected in Sector 4. Drafting highly probable. Primary pumps at max RPM.'). No intervention actions logged. No manual overrides. No isolation staging. No communication with external parties documented.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: NO GAP DETECTED. Operators are doing exactly what their protocol says they should do at current pressure levels (41.2 PSI, well above the 20 PSI intervention threshold). Per LZ-EH-003, their action (passive monitoring) is consistent with their stated protocol. The question is not whether operators are failing their protocol, but whether the protocol itself is adequate for the approaching state transition. That is a design question, not an operator behavior question.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: Protocol-compliant wait-and-monitor. The automated-first architecture explicitly envisions operators as secondary to SCADA logic. Operators are doing what the system design expects them to do. No say-do gap to exploit analytically.

player_analysis.stated_position: No direct statement available. Operator log infers 'Drafting highly probable' but this is second-hand inference, not direct communication.
player_analysis.observed_actions: Observable ONLY through system-level effects: 19,500 GPM flow, Sector 4 pressure at 41.2 PSI (depressed relative to grid average of 50.1 PSI), pumps at 98.5% load. The demand source is pulling approximately 8.9 PSI more than the grid average from Sector 4 (50.1 - 41.2 = 8.9 PSI differential). This is a large, sustained draw concentrated in one sector.
player_analysis.say_do_gap: CANNOT ASSESS — no 'say' data exists. Per CL-005 and CL-013, no FD-operator coordination can be assumed. The demand source is a black box whose only observable signature is its effect on the grid. We can measure what it's TAKING but not what it INTENDS or how long it will take.
player_analysis.inferred_strategy: INSUFFICIENT_EVIDENCE to infer strategy. Fire operations (if that is indeed the demand source) are driven by the fire, not by the water grid. The demand source's behavior is exogenous to the system — it does not respond to grid conditions, pressure levels, or SCADA logic. This asymmetry is the fundamental strategic challenge: the grid must adapt to a demand source that is indifferent to the grid's constraints.

feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Any increase in demand pushes pump load toward or beyond 100%, which accelerates cavitation from WARNING_ELEVATED toward CRITICAL_DAMAGE, which either triggers SCADA pump trip (per thesis) or triggers Kill Switch #5 (if SCADA fails to trip during 'sustained' WARNING_ELEVATED). The loop accelerates because: more demand → more pump strain → more cavitation → closer to forced shutdown. The system has zero headroom (98.5% load) so ANY demand increase enters the critical zone immediately.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This loop is the mechanism that will force the thesis's moment of truth. The pump trip is not a possibility — it is the inevitable terminus of this loop if demand persists or increases. The thesis DEPENDS on this loop resolving via SCADA protective shutdown followed by successful gravity handoff. The loop is self-terminating only if: (a) demand drops, (b) SCADA trips pumps, or (c) pumps mechanically fail. Options (b) and (c) both trigger the gravity handoff that is the thesis's core claim.

feedback_loops.loop: Pump Trip → Pressure Drop → Gravity Deployment Transition
feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: SCADA pump trip activates this loop. Sequence: pumps stop → immediate pressure drop in Sector 4 → pressure falls toward gravity tank static head → gravity flow begins → pressure stabilizes (thesis success) OR pressure drops below 20 PSI before gravity engages (thesis falsification via KS-2). Simultaneously, sudden pump cessation creates water hammer risk → surge relief valve must actuate. This loop has NOT yet activated. Its behavior when activated is the single largest uncertainty in the assessment.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: This is the thesis itself, expressed as a feedback loop. The thesis claims this loop resolves with pressure stabilization above 20 PSI. Falsification occurs if the loop resolves with pressure below 20 PSI. The loop's behavior depends on unknowns: gravity tank head pressure, surge valve actuation, demand persistence during transition. Currently dormant — all stress is in the precursor loop (Demand-Pump-Cavitation).

feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: If Sector 4 is hydraulically connected to the broader grid AND the pump trip causes a large pressure transient, the pressure wave could propagate to adjacent sectors. This would be visible as grid_average_pressure declining. Currently, the 8.9 PSI differential (50.1 - 41.2) suggests stress IS localized and the grid is providing some buffering. However, per SIG-014, we do not know the grid interconnect architecture. If Sector 4 is islanded, contagion risk is zero. If highly connected, contagion risk exists but the grid's 50.1 PSI average provides substantial buffer.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: If stress remains localized (current evidence suggests it is), Kill Switch #1 (system-wide collapse) remains far from triggering. If contagion occurs, the crisis escalates from a single-sector event to a system-wide event, dramatically changing the assessment. Per LZ-RC-003, we cannot determine which scenario applies without interconnect data.

feedback_loops.trigger_to_next_state: Activates only AFTER pump trip. Once gravity reserves are the sole supply, depletion rate becomes the critical metric. If demand continues at current levels (19,500 GPM) and reserves are the only source, the time to depletion depends on total reserve volume (UNKNOWN). Kill Switch #4 triggers at <10% with pumps offline. This loop is a countdown timer that starts when pumps trip. The 98% starting level is favorable but meaningless without knowing total volume.
feedback_loops.thesis_implication: The thesis implicitly assumes reserves last long enough for either demand to subside or operators to intervene. If total reserve volume is small relative to the 19,500 GPM draw rate, the countdown could be minutes, not hours. This is a critical gap that becomes actionable only after the pump trip.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: SCADA has not yet tripped pumps despite WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: SCADA trip logic is functioning normally — WARNING_ELEVATED is within the normal pre-trip operating envelope, and the trip threshold is either CRITICAL_DAMAGE or a duration timer at WARNING_ELEVATED that has not yet expired. The system is behaving exactly as designed; our characterization of 'ruthless' in the thesis overstated the immediacy of the response.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: If strategic: SCADA logic may prioritize service continuity longer than expected, accepting pump damage risk to maintain pressure during a life-safety event (fire). This would suggest the logic has a 'fire mode' or service-priority override.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: SCADA trip logic has a firmware defect or miscalibration that delays protective shutdown beyond safe operating limits.
evidence_citations: Direct telemetry: pumps at 98.5% load with WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation (SIG-001, SIG-002)
Thesis documentation characterizes SCADA as 'ruthlessly' protective but does not specify exact trip parameters, thresholds, or timing
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Next telemetry reading: either (a) pumps trip and cavitation drops from WARNING_ELEVATED confirming normal protective sequence, (b) pumps remain on and cavitation escalates to CRITICAL_DAMAGE indicating delayed or failed protection, or (c) pumps remain on and cavitation stays at WARNING_ELEVATED indicating duration timer still counting.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis is most likely because: (1) per LZ-RC-004, the simplest sufficient explanation must be tested first; (2) the thesis's use of 'ruthlessly' is a characterization, not a specification — SCADA may indeed be ruthless but on its own timeline, not ours; (3) WARNING_ELEVATED is explicitly described as the 'penultimate' state, implying there IS a state above it (CRITICAL_DAMAGE) that may be the actual trip trigger; (4) industrial SCADA systems routinely include grace periods to avoid false trips from transient spikes. Per CL-001/CL-004/CL-011: I will not infer SCADA's internal logic state.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: System total flow approaching catastrophic leak threshold while reserves remain high
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The high flow (19,500 GPM) is entirely explained by the massive demand event (probable fire drafting). The combination of high flow with stable reserves (98%) is internally consistent: the pumps are meeting the demand, so reserves haven't been tapped. There is no leak — flow is high because demand is high.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: A secondary leak exists somewhere in the system that is contributing to the high flow, masked by the obvious fire drafting event.
evidence_citations: System flow at 19,500 GPM with operator log confirming 'massive sudden draw' (SIG-003)
Gravity reserves at 98% indicating pumps are meeting demand without reserve deployment (SIG-004)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: If reserves begin dropping while pumps remain active and at max capacity, it would indicate demand is exceeding pump capacity — which is consistent with the demand event, not a separate leak. A leak would be indicated by reserves dropping AND flow exceeding demand estimates AND pumps active — but we have no demand estimate to compare against.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: Per LZ-RC-004: the simplest explanation (high demand event causes high flow) is sufficient. Kill Switch #3 requires three simultaneous conditions; only one is near-met (flow near 20,000 GPM). Reserves at 98% and their stability strongly suggest pumps are keeping up with demand, which is inconsistent with an uncontained leak scenario where flow would exceed pump + reserve capacity.

strategic_inferences.finding_from_layer2: Imminent state transition: pump trip and gravity handoff
strategic_inferences.null_hypothesis: The state transition may not be as imminent as it appears. If demand subsides (fire knocked down, operations scaled back), pump load drops, cavitation resolves, and no transition occurs. The system returns to normal without testing the thesis.
strategic_inferences.rational_explanation: Given current observable conditions — 98.5% pump load, WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation, 19,500 GPM flow — the system is in a state that resolves one of three ways: (1) demand drops and stress resolves without transition; (2) SCADA trips pumps and gravity handoff occurs; (3) pumps mechanically fail and gravity handoff occurs forcibly. Two of three resolution paths involve the state transition. The question is whether demand drops before SCADA acts or pumps fail.
strategic_inferences.alternative_explanation: Demand is already at peak and will begin declining, making the transition unnecessary. This is possible but not evidenced — we have no data on demand trajectory per SIG-009.
assumptions: Demand will persist at current or higher levels long enough for SCADA trip logic to activate OR for cavitation to reach CRITICAL_DAMAGE
evidence_citations: Pump load at 98.5% with zero headroom (SIG-001, verified by two independent sources per LZ-EH-001)
Cavitation at WARNING_ELEVATED, penultimate state (SIG-002, direct sensor measurement)
strategic_inferences.materialization_signal: Pump status changing to OFFLINE in next telemetry reading, accompanied by pressure drop in Sector 4 and gravity reserve percentage beginning to decline.
strategic_inferences.reasoning: The null hypothesis (demand drops before transition) is possible but requires an assumption about demand trajectory that we have no evidence for. The primary explanation (system is approaching a forced transition) is supported by two verified, independent Tier 1 measurements (pump load, cavitation) that are both at penultimate stress levels. Per LZ-EH-003, SCADA's observable action (continuing to run pumps at damaging conditions) suggests the trip threshold has not yet been reached, but the trajectory is clearly toward that threshold if demand persists. One assumption (demand persistence) is required, making this VALID.

This is normal, expected behavior — the valve will become relevant when/if pumps trip, not during the current demand surge.

strategic_inferences.reasoning: Per LZ-RC-004, the simplest explanation is sufficient. The valve is a reactive device for pump-stop events. Pumps haven't stopped. The valve is correctly in its default state. The alternative (malfunction) has zero supporting evidence — per LZ-RC-003, absence of actuation is not evidence of malfunction when the actuation condition hasn't occurred.

If SCADA trips pumps and gravity deployment bridges the gap above 20 PSI in Sector 4, the thesis is confirmed — the system sacrificed active components and maintained viability through passive reserves, exactly as designed.
thesis_confirmed.timeline: Minutes to hours. Confirmation requires observing the pump trip AND the gravity handoff maintaining Sector 4 above 20 PSI. If demand subsides before pump trip, the thesis is confirmed by default (system weathered the stress) but the core mechanism (gravity handoff) remains untested.

thesis_modified.modification: The thesis's characterization of SCADA as 'ruthlessly' protective may need modification to 'protective with tolerance bands' or 'protective at higher damage thresholds than expected.' The system may accept more equipment damage than the thesis implies before sacrificing service. Additionally, the transition dynamics may reveal that the gravity handoff is slower or less complete than the thesis implies — resulting in a temporary pressure dip below comfortable levels but still above 20 PSI. The thesis holds in principle but the margins are thinner than described.
thesis_modified.key_evidence: SCADA's continued operation at WARNING_ELEVATED suggests trip parameters are less aggressive than 'ruthless' implies (SIG-008). The unknown gravity tank head pressure (SIG-012) creates uncertainty about how quickly gravity flow replaces pump flow. The surge valve's untested response (SIG-007) adds transition risk.

thesis_falsified.key_evidence: Two kill switches at WARNING status simultaneously (KS-3, KS-5). System at zero headroom with unknown demand trajectory. Multiple compounding unknowns in the transition mechanism (SCADA trip timing, tank head pressure, surge valve response, demand persistence). If the pump-to-gravity transition produces a pressure drop below 20 PSI in Sector 4 — even briefly — the thesis is falsified on its contamination criterion. The 25% reflects: (a) the genuine uncertainty about transition dynamics, (b) the unknown demand trajectory, (c) the possibility that gravity reserves deploy too slowly to prevent a pressure excursion below 20 PSI.
thesis_falsified.confirming_signal: Next telemetry reading showing Sector 4 pressure below 25 PSI (approaching KS-2), or cavitation escalating to CRITICAL_DAMAGE without pump trip (KS-5 triggered), or reserves dropping >15%/min with high flow and active pumps (KS-3 triggered).

compound_stress_inference.orderly_vs_disorderly: Currently ORDERLY but approaching the boundary. Stress is visible, measurable, and the system is responding within its designed parameters (pumps at max, operators monitoring, no kill switches triggered). The transition from orderly to disorderly would occur at the pump trip event — this is a discontinuous state change where the system must hand off from active to passive supply. If the handoff works, stress returns to orderly (managed depletion of reserves). If the handoff fails (pressure drops below 20 PSI), stress becomes disorderly (contamination risk, regulatory violation, potential cascade). The current state is 'orderly stress approaching a cliff edge' — the cliff is the pump trip.
compound_stress_inference.catalyst_proximity: The catalyst for the transition from orderly to potentially disorderly is the SCADA pump trip. Proximity: CLOSE but temporally undefined. Preconditions are met (98.5% load, WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation). The trip will occur when either: (a) SCADA's internal logic threshold is crossed (unknown timing), (b) cavitation reaches CRITICAL_DAMAGE (unknown timing), or (c) demand drops and the crisis resolves without a trip. The catalyst is close in parameter-space (conditions are near threshold) but its temporal proximity depends on SCADA logic we cannot observe.
compound_stress_inference.pre_load_assessment: The system is pre-loaded on the MECHANICAL dimension: pumps at 98.5% with WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation. This is the 'loaded spring' that will release as a pump trip. The system is NOT pre-loaded on the RESERVE dimension: 98% gravity reserves provide substantial buffer. The system is NOT pre-loaded on the PRESSURE dimension: 41.2 PSI in Sector 4 and 50.1 PSI grid average provide substantial buffers above kill switch thresholds. The asymmetry of pre-loading (mechanical stress high, reserve and pressure buffers high) is exactly the thesis's design intent: the system sacrifices the mechanical component while the passive buffers absorb the transition.
compound_stress_inference.thesis_paradox: The approaching pump trip is simultaneously the HIGHEST-RISK event and the HIGHEST-VALIDATION event for the thesis. If SCADA trips pumps and gravity reserves maintain Sector 4 above 20 PSI, the thesis is powerfully confirmed — the system did exactly what it was designed to do under extreme stress. If the transition fails and pressure drops below 20 PSI, the thesis is falsified at its core claim. There is no middle ground during the transition: the thesis either works or it doesn't. The system is approaching the moment where this paradox resolves. Every minute at current stress levels brings the resolution closer. The bear pressure of 7 reflects this genuine uncertainty: the thesis's safety margins are holding (supporting the thesis) while the system approaches a discontinuous event whose outcome depends on unknowns (threatening the thesis). An analyst who is ONLY bullish ignores the transition risk. An analyst who is ONLY bearish ignores the substantial buffers. The honest assessment is: we are approaching a binary test of the thesis under conditions that are both more favorable (98% reserves, 41.2 PSI buffer) and more uncertain (unknown SCADA logic, unknown tank head pressure, unknown demand trajectory) than anticipated.

_layer3_raw.inference_summary: Player behavior reveals a system operating exactly as designed under extreme stress — but approaching a discontinuous state transition whose outcome depends on parameters invisible to the current data feed. SCADA's continued operation of pumps at WARNING_ELEVATED cavitation is the key behavioral signal: it is either normal pre-trip sequencing (benign) or failure to protect equipment (critical), and the distinction depends on trip logic parameters we cannot observe. All three hidden moves categories (SCADA internal state, operator off-log activities, FD coordination) have been correctly and repeatedly rejected by the corrections ledger — the honest assessment is that we cannot see inside any of these black boxes. Layer 4 should focus on: (1) whether the next telemetry reading shows cavitation escalation, pump status change, or reserve depletion — any of these resolves the current ambiguity; (2) the SCADA trip logic parameter gap as the single highest-value intelligence target; (3) maintaining the paradox framing — the thesis's moment of truth is approaching, and the evidence genuinely supports both a successful and failed outcome at this pre-transition point.
